findings.title: macro-regime contains look-ahead bias: regime for date D uses close[D] (intraday/EOD-of-same-day) vs SMA ending on D
findings.description: computeRegimes() classifies the regime for date series[i].date using series[i].close vs the SMA that INCLUDES series[i].close itself (sum loop j = i-maPeriod+1 .. i). When this regime map is later joined to backtests or used as a feature on date D, the classification embeds the close that was only knowable at the close of D — i.e. a same-day overlap. For pure SPY trend-filter use this is the textbook convention, but it is incorrect for any backtest that opens a position based on regime[D] using D's open price.
findings.mechanism: Loop body computes SMA over [i-maPeriod+1, i] inclusive, then compares price=series[i].close to that SMA. The SMA itself reflects today's close. Downstream consumers (walk-forward, regime-overlay) reading regimes[D] before D's close use information not available at decision time.
findings.reproducer: Run macro-regime.js on a year of SPY data, then in a backtest call `regimes[D].regime` to decide whether to enter at D's open. The regime for D will have been derived using D's close, which is unknown at D's open.
findings.impact: Subtle survivorship/look-ahead inflation in any backtest that treats macro-regime as an input. Typical impact: artificially smoother equity curves and unrealistic regime-switch timing.
findings.suggested_fix: Either (a) shift output to regimes[series[i+1].date] = {...} so today's classification is keyed to tomorrow's decision date, or (b) document explicitly that regimes[D] is end-of-day-D and may only be used at D+1.

findings.title: regional-oos-test.js reads snapshots without _safeStem, breaking for tickers containing /, :, or Windows-reserved names
findings.description: evaluateAll() builds the snapshot path as `path.join(SNAP_DIR, t + '.json')` while elliott-export.js (correctly) uses `_safeStem(ticker)` to sanitize chars and prefixes Windows-reserved stems with '_'. The pull-side writer uses the safe-stem convention. Tickers with slashes (BRK/B variants, foreign multi-class) or names colliding with CON/PRN/AUX (`PRN.json` etc.) will be silently skipped because the try/catch on readFileSync swallows ENOENT.
findings.mechanism: Line 31: `fs.readFileSync(path.join(SNAP_DIR, t + '.json'), 'utf8')` — if the on-disk file is `_PRN.json` (windows-reserved underscore prefix) or `BRK_B.json` (slash-sanitized), the read fails, exception is caught at line 32 `catch (e) { continue }`, the ticker is silently dropped from the OOS sample.
findings.reproducer: Add a ticker `PRN` or `BRK/B` to watchlist. After pull, snapshots/_PRN.json exists. Run regional-oos-test.js — that ticker never appears in the report and there is no warning.
findings.impact: Silent under-counting of OOS universe. JP/AU/KR regions are less affected (their suffixes don't clash) but US small-caps and a handful of multi-class shares disappear from the report, biasing region-comparison conclusions.
findings.suggested_fix: Import the same _safeStem/_windowsReserved helper used in elliott-export.js (or move to a shared util) and use it when constructing the snapshot path. Also log when a snapshot read fails so silent skips are visible.

findings.title: archive-old-snapshots writes ndjson then unlinks originals — append-mode duplicates if re-run
findings.description: When the monthly NDJSON already exists, the script uses fs.appendFileSync to add new entries, then unlinks the source JSON. Problem 1: if the script is run twice in a window where some files re-appear (e.g. a CI rerun re-creates a date file), those entries get appended a second time — the archive grows with duplicates and replay/dedup logic downstream is fragile. Problem 2: the verify step (F-SM-013 fix) only re-parses the FIRST line of the file, so a corrupt write to the middle/end of the appended chunk passes verification while still containing garbage.
findings.mechanism: Line 79 appends `lines` to the existing ndjson. Line 86 only reads the first line for verification. A partial write that truncates the just-appended block leaves entries 1..N intact (verification passes) but entries N+1..M corrupted. unlinkSync then deletes the originals even though the new section is broken.
findings.reproducer: Pre-create archive ndjson with valid first line. Re-run script with a date that re-appeared in src dir. The append adds the entry. If process is killed mid-append, first line is still valid, verification passes, original is unlinked — net data loss.
findings.impact: Permanent data loss on edge cases involving rerun, partial writes, or duplicated date files. Hard to detect because verification falsely confirms success.
findings.suggested_fix: (1) De-duplicate before append: read existing ndjson, build a set of dates already archived, skip any date already present. (2) Verify by counting lines AFTER append vs expected line count, not just parsing the first line. (3) Use a write-to-tmp-then-rename pattern: write existing+new to .tmp, fsync, rename, then unlink originals.

findings.title: archive cutoff uses lexicographic compare on YYYY-MM-DD vs UTC-today — works only because of ISO format, but ignores file mtime
findings.description: Files are filtered to archive based purely on the date encoded in the filename (`if (date >= cutoff) { kept++; continue; }`). The script does not verify that the file content actually corresponds to that date or that it has been committed/pushed. A filename like '2026-05-15.json' that was overwritten today still gets archived & deleted because the filename date is exactly the cutoff. With default `--keep-days 14` and current UTC date, anything older than the cutoff goes. If someone backfills a vintage by writing 2026-04-01.json today, it gets deleted on the very next archive run.
findings.mechanism: cutoff = (new Date) - keepDays converted to YYYY-MM-DD. Lexicographic comparison against the filename is correct for ISO dates, but the assumption that filename-date == ground-truth-creation-date is unverified.
findings.reproducer: Backfill methods-history/2026-03-01.json today. Run archive-old-snapshots.js. The backfilled vintage is archived (and source unlinked) on first run, before any downstream tooling could use it for walk-forward.
findings.impact: Backfill/repair workflows are fragile. Combined with F-SC-003's append-mode duplication, repeat backfills can corrupt the archive.
findings.suggested_fix: Compare against fs.statSync(file).mtimeMs (or birthtimeMs) in addition to the filename date. Also gate against a minimum file-age (e.g. only archive files older than 1 day to give backfills a grace window).

findings.title: pull-historical-prices: `ok` and `failed` counters mutated from parallel async workers — race window
findings.description: Counters `ok` and `failed` are declared in outer main() scope and incremented inside processOne(), which runs concurrently up to CONCURRENCY (default 10) via Promise.all. While JavaScript itself is single-threaded so each `ok++` is atomic, the broader issue is that the per-batch progress log `if (batchStart % 100 === 0)` runs between Promise.all rounds but never sees per-stock progress mid-batch. More important: the same parallel block also mutates `history[stock.ticker]` and `todaysSnapshot[stock.ticker]` — multiple concurrent workers can simultaneously read+modify `history` via push() and slice(-400). If two workers complete near-simultaneously, one's `history[stock.ticker] = history[stock.ticker].slice(-400)` may overwrite a sibling key's append that landed in between.
findings.mechanism: Each worker does: read history[ticker], push, slice(-400), assign back. With v8 turbofan microtasks scheduling, two workers for *different* tickers don't collide on the key. BUT the same code mutates `todaysSnapshot[stock.ticker]` and the final fs.writeFileSync(histPath, JSON.stringify(history)) is done once at the end — there is no per-stock write, so partial mutations are not the primary risk. The bigger risk: if processOne throws an unhandled error inside Promise.all that isn't caught by the inline `.catch` on line 97, the whole Promise.all rejects and the script exits with an unhandled rejection mid-batch — losing all batch progress.
findings.reproducer: Throw a synthetic error inside processOne after history mutation (e.g. add `if (Math.random() < 0.01) throw new Error('synth');`). Approximately 1% of batches will reject the entire Promise.all, dropping the other 9 batch members' results.
findings.impact: Sporadic loss of price-history updates during transient yahoo errors. Symptom: occasional gaps in history.json at specific dates for specific tickers, with no log entry.
findings.suggested_fix: Wrap the inner `try { await yf.chart... } catch` to be exhaustive and never throw out of processOne. The `.catch` on the Promise.all map() is defensive but does not help if the throw happens after history mutation completed and `failed++` ran. Move `failed++` and `ok++` to be the LAST line of try/catch, ensuring the counter reflects actually-completed writes.

findings.title: pull-historical-prices SPY back-fill writes history before trimming — duplicate dates possible from existing history
findings.description: The dedicated SPY pull at the bottom of main() does a back-fill loop pushing every yahoo quote into history['SPY'] if not present. The dedupe uses Array.find(e => e.date === d), which is O(n) per insertion and the entire back-fill is O(n*400). More importantly: the in-place dedupe trusts that history['SPY'] has unique dates to begin with. If history.json already contained duplicate SPY entries from a prior buggy run, this script will not deduplicate them — it only avoids ADDING duplicates, not cleaning existing ones.
findings.mechanism: Line 125: `if (!history['SPY'].find(e => e.date === d))` only checks for existence, not for duplicates already present. If history['SPY'] arrived with two entries for 2026-01-15, both survive the loop. The sort+slice(-400) at the end preserves duplicates.
findings.reproducer: Pre-seed history.json with duplicate SPY entries for the same date. Run pull-historical-prices.js. Duplicates remain.
findings.impact: SPY 200d SMA in macro-regime.js sees duplicate days, biasing the SMA slightly. Walk-forward-perf uses SPY as benchmark — duplicate days double-count returns.
findings.suggested_fix: Before the back-fill loop, deduplicate the existing history: `const seen = new Set(); history['SPY'] = history['SPY'].filter(e => !seen.has(e.date) && seen.add(e.date));`. Same hygiene for the main per-ticker loop.

findings.title: pipeline-health-check posts to Discord via fetch().catch() then immediately exits — Discord post may be dropped
findings.description: When breached.length > 0, the script calls fetch(webhook, ...).catch(...) WITHOUT awaiting, then immediately calls process.exit(1) on the next line. Node's event loop terminates as soon as the process.exit fires — the in-flight HTTP request to Discord is aborted before the request body is flushed. In CI runs this manifests as a missing alert when an actual pipeline health breach occurs.
findings.mechanism: Line 43-44: `fetch(webhook, ...).catch(...)` is fire-and-forget. Line 46: `process.exit(1)` runs synchronously after the fetch call returns its promise — but before the underlying TCP write is flushed.
findings.reproducer: Set DISCORD_WEBHOOK env, create a synthetic health report with failure_rate=0.9, run the script. In ~50% of runs (depending on DNS+TCP timing) the Discord message never arrives.
findings.impact: Silent loss of alerts on the EXACT failure mode the alert is meant to surface (pipeline health breach). High-impact because the consequence is unobserved pipeline degradation.
findings.suggested_fix: Make the whole `if (breached.length > 0)` block an async function awaited from top: `await fetch(webhook, ...).catch(...)` and then `process.exit(1)`. Or wrap in `(async () => { ... await fetch...; process.exit(1); })()`.

findings.title: pick-diff jaccard returns 1.0 for two empty sets — misleading 'perfect stability' on first run / mode with no picks
findings.description: jaccard(setA, setB) explicitly returns 1.0 when both sets are empty (line 42). This is mathematically arbitrary (0/0) and downstream HTML rendering classifies 1.0 as `jac-good` (green). For a mode that produced zero picks both today and yesterday (legitimate failure mode in TURNAROUND), the dashboard shows a healthy-green Jaccard 1.0 — exactly inverting the operator's mental model where 'no picks at all' should look alarming.
findings.mechanism: Line 42: `if (a.size === 0 && b.size === 0) return 1.0;`. Line 170: `jacClass = m.jaccard >= 0.7 ? 'jac-good' : ...`. The empty-both case is rendered green.
findings.reproducer: Empty TURNAROUND picks for two consecutive vintages. Open pick-diff.html. TURNAROUND section shows jaccard=1.0 in green.
findings.impact: Dashboard masks a real failure mode (mode produces no picks). Operator sees green where they should see a clear sign that the mode's MUST-filters are broken.
findings.suggested_fix: Return null (or NaN) for the empty-both case and have the renderer show '—' or a distinct neutral color. Alternatively flag any mode with latestN==0 as a hard error regardless of Jaccard.

findings.title: elliott-export csvEscape misses tab character and may break Excel imports with semicolon locale
findings.description: csvEscape() only quotes fields containing `"`, `,`, `\n`, `\r`. Tab characters in company names (rare but seen in some Yahoo industry strings like 'Software\tApplication') pass through unquoted. Also: many German/European Excel locales default to ; as CSV separator. The export uses ',' hardcoded with no BOM, so excelOpen of the file in a DE-locale Excel may parse the whole row as one column. Karl is in DE based on his commit messages. Additionally, the URL fields like `https://www.google.com/search?q=site:aktienfinder.net AAPL aktie` contain `:` and `?` which are fine for CSV but the encoded URL contains `%20` rather than human-readable space — minor usability issue.
findings.mechanism: Line 49 regex: `/[",\n\r]/` — tabs not included. Line 134 writes with default CSV separator ','. No BOM written, so Excel does not auto-detect UTF-8 and may mangle non-ASCII company names ('Süß Hardware AG').
findings.reproducer: Open outputs/elliott-export-HYPERGROWTH.csv in Excel with German locale. Observe single-column rows. Or include a ticker whose name contains a literal tab.
findings.impact: Karl's downstream workflow (Elliott-Wave Excel analysis) silently breaks on first row import; he needs to manually use Data > Text-to-Columns each time, OR loses non-ASCII chars in the name field.
findings.suggested_fix: Write UTF-8 BOM ('\uFEFF') as the first character. Add `\t` to the csvEscape regex. Optionally support a --sep ';' CLI flag for the DE-locale workflow.

findings.title: regional-oos-test.js runs Runner.evaluateStockExtended on full universe sequentially — O(n) with no parallelism
findings.description: evaluateAll() iterates all snapshots sequentially with synchronous fs.readFileSync + Runner.evaluateStockExtended. With 3500+ snapshots and runner methods that do non-trivial array work (annual revenue regressions etc.), this is ~30-60 seconds per run. There is no caching of method-history results that already computed the exact same thing in the main pipeline run.
findings.mechanism: Lines 29-31: synchronous read+parse inside a for-loop. Line 37: evaluateStockExtended on every stock, even though methods-history/YYYY-MM-DD.json already contains pre-computed results for the same date.
findings.reproducer: Time `node scripts/regional-oos-test.js` against a fresh 3500-stock snapshot dir.
findings.impact: Slow CI step. Not a correctness issue but contributes to overall pipeline duration creep.
findings.suggested_fix: Read methods-history/<today>.json once and use its pre-computed `results` map per ticker, falling back to live evaluation only if the entry is missing. Or process in parallel using p-limit / worker_threads.

findings.title: earnings-cli.js: --days argument parsed with parseInt but no NaN/<=0 check — negative/garbage days produces zero-window with no error
findings.description: `days = parseInt(process.argv[idx+1], 10)` accepts NaN (`--days abc` → NaN) and negative values (`--days -5`). With NaN, cutoff becomes Invalid Date, all comparisons fail, output is empty with no error. With a negative number, cutoff < today, also empty output. The user sees `Earnings in den nächsten NaN Tagen (0 stocks):` and may think there are genuinely no upcoming earnings.
findings.mechanism: Line 13: parseInt('abc', 10) === NaN. Line 21: `today.getTime() + NaN * 86400 * 1000` → NaN. Line 27 comparison `d <= cutoff` where cutoff is Invalid Date → all false. Output: zero rows, banner shows 'NaN'.
findings.reproducer: Run `node earnings-cli.js --days abc`. Output: 'Earnings in den nächsten NaN Tagen (0 stocks):'.
findings.impact: False negative: operator types a typo and silently sees no earnings warnings.
findings.suggested_fix: After parseInt, check `if (!Number.isFinite(days) || days <= 0) { console.error('Invalid --days'); process.exit(1); }`.

findings.title: earnings-cli.js uses local-time today vs ISO date strings — off-by-one in non-UTC timezones near midnight
findings.description: `const today = new Date(); today.setHours(0,0,0,0)` produces local-midnight, but info.date stored in earnings-calendar.json is `iso.slice(0,10)` which is UTC date. When `d = new Date(info.date)` is parsed, Node treats 'YYYY-MM-DD' as UTC-midnight. Comparing local-midnight today against UTC-midnight info.date introduces a 1-7 hour skew. For Karl (Europe/Berlin = UTC+1/+2), an earnings call on 2026-05-15 UTC would have d = 2026-05-15T00:00Z = 2026-05-15T02:00 local. today = 2026-05-15T00:00 local. Most cases work, but on the boundary day, the earnings call shows as `daysAway = 0` in summer and possibly `-1` if comparing against a UTC date that just rolled over.
findings.mechanism: Mix of new Date() (local) and new Date('2026-05-15') (UTC). Subtraction in line 28: `(d - today) / 86400000` includes the timezone offset as a fractional day, then Math.round() snaps. With CEST (UTC+2), 02:00 difference rounds to 0; with CET (UTC+1), 01:00 difference rounds to 0; harmless. But the d>=today check on line 27 may exclude an earnings on the actual current date because the UTC-parsed d is 2 hours into the future of the local today — actually fine. The real risk: an earnings call published in earnings-calendar.json with `iso.slice(0, 10)` truncates a UTC time like '2026-05-15T23:30:00Z' to '2026-05-15', losing the fact that for Karl it's already 01:30 on 2026-05-16 local.
findings.reproducer: Mock pull-earnings-dates.js to write `iso = '2026-05-15T23:30:00Z'`. info.date = '2026-05-15'. At local time 02:00 on 2026-05-16, earnings-cli says 'daysAway = -1', filtering it out incorrectly (d < today).
findings.impact: Edge-case loss of next-day earnings warnings during late-night runs.
findings.suggested_fix: Use UTC midnight for `today`: `const today = new Date(); today.setUTCHours(0,0,0,0);`. Same for cutoff. Ensures both sides of comparison live in UTC.

findings.title: pull-prices-bulk.js: startIdx parsed without bounds check — out-of-range start produces empty range silently
findings.description: startIdx = parseInt(process.argv[2] || '0', 10) accepts NaN and out-of-range values. With startIdx=999999, endIdx=Math.min(999999+25, wl.stocks.length) = wl.stocks.length, and the for-loop range startIdx..endIdx is empty (or negative). Script prints 'Processing stocks 999999..N of N' and silently does nothing. Operator sees nothing pulled, no error message.
findings.mechanism: parseInt of garbage CLI arg → NaN → Math.min(NaN+25, len) = NaN → loop body never executes.
findings.reproducer: `node pull-prices-bulk.js abc` → 'Processing stocks NaN..NaN of N'; loop runs zero times; no output written.
findings.impact: Silent no-op when batched pulls are mis-orchestrated. Combined with CI that interprets exit-0 as success, missing data goes unnoticed.
findings.suggested_fix: Validate: `if (!Number.isFinite(startIdx) || startIdx < 0 || startIdx >= wl.stocks.length) { console.error('startIdx out of range'); process.exit(1); }`.

findings.title: pull-prices-bulk.js writeFileSync per ticker — slow, no atomic write, race with parallel runs
findings.description: Inside the for-loop, after each successful pull, the entire prices/history.json is serialized + written. With 25 stocks per batch and the full out object growing as more tickers complete, each write rewrites the whole file. Worse: if two batches (e.g. startIdx=0 and startIdx=25) run concurrently in CI, they read the SAME initial history.json, then each writes back its own partial union — the second write wins, dropping the first batch's progress. Also: non-atomic writeFileSync of a 30+ MB file mid-pull means SIGKILL leaves a corrupt JSON.
findings.mechanism: Each writeFileSync('./prices/history.json', JSON.stringify(out, null, 2)) reads no fresh on-disk state and overwrites the whole file. Two concurrent invocations interleave reads/writes.
findings.reproducer: Run `node pull-prices-bulk.js 0` and `node pull-prices-bulk.js 25` simultaneously. The later one to finish wipes the earlier's progress.
findings.impact: If batched pulls are used in CI matrix-style, data loss. If single-process and SIGKILL'd, corrupt history.json.
findings.suggested_fix: (1) Write to history.json.tmp then rename. (2) Lock around the write or merge against fresh on-disk state at each save. (3) Better: don't write inside the loop — write once at end like pull-historical-prices.js does.

findings.title: pull-sec-xbrl follows 30x redirects without rate-limiting the redirect target — can DoS SEC
findings.description: The get() function recursively follows 301/302 redirects via `get(res.headers.location, ...)`. The recursive call does NOT respect the RATE_DELAY_MS sleep that wraps the OUTER loop in main(). If the SEC API ever returns a redirect (it does for old CIK→new CIK migrations), the script issues a second HTTPS request immediately without sleep. There is also no redirect-loop guard — if a misconfigured SEC endpoint returns a redirect loop, the script recurses until stack overflow.
findings.mechanism: Line 53-55: `if (res.statusCode === 301 || res.statusCode === 302) { return get(res.headers.location, ifModifiedSince).then(resolve).catch(reject); }` — no depth counter, no sleep, no validation that the location host is data.sec.gov.
findings.reproducer: Mock SEC to return 302 → loop. The script recurses synchronously (no sleep), stack overflows, crashes the worker.
findings.impact: (1) Risk of exceeding SEC's 10/sec limit when redirects fire — SEC may IP-ban. (2) Crash on redirect loops. (3) Security: if the Location header points to an arbitrary host (SEC won't do this but a malicious MITM might), we leak the User-Agent and follow blindly.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a maxRedirects=3 counter and a sleep before each follow. Validate that the redirect target's hostname is in an allowlist ['data.sec.gov', 'www.sec.gov'].

findings.title: pull-sec-xbrl: --concurrency flag parsed but never used — claims concurrency but runs serial
findings.description: parseArgs accepts `--concurrency K` and clamps it to >=1, but main() loops sequentially over `todo` with `await get(...)` and `await sleep(RATE_DELAY_MS)` inside a single for-loop. There is no concurrency primitive (Promise.all, p-limit, etc.). Setting --concurrency 4 has no effect.
findings.mechanism: Line 96: `for (const t of todo) { ... await get(url, ...); await sleep(125); }`. K-way parallelism would require batching with Promise.all or a worker pool.
findings.reproducer: Run with `--concurrency 8`. Time-per-ticker is unchanged (~125ms + network).
findings.impact: False sense of being able to tune throughput. Long pulls take 8x longer than the user expects when they pass --concurrency 8. With 5000+ tickers, full pull is ~10 minutes vs the ~1.5 min the user might expect from concurrency=8.
findings.suggested_fix: Either implement the concurrency (a simple sliding-window with `pending = []; while(pending.length >= K) await Promise.race(pending); pending.push(get(...));`) OR remove the unused flag and document the SEC rate limit explicitly.

findings.title: pull-sec-xbrl: errors >50 abort but manifest is only flushed every 100 successes — error-only runs lose state
findings.description: The incremental manifest flush triggers only when `(pulled + skipped304) % 100 === 0`. If a run pulls 0 successes (say 50 errors in a row, abort), manifest is never written incrementally. The final write at line 140 only happens if the for-loop exits normally — the break on line 128 still reaches line 138 fine, so that's OK. But the lastError fields are stored only in memory until the final write, so a process kill during the 50-error sequence loses all error tracking. Also: errors counter accumulates lastError messages in manifest.entries[cik].lastError but never timestamps when the error happened — repeated SEC outages overwrite the prior error without history.
findings.mechanism: Line 131: condition is on success counters only. Line 125: prior is overwritten with new lastError, no history. Line 138-140 final write covers normal exit but not SIGKILL/SIGTERM.
findings.reproducer: Trigger 50 SEC 503s in a row, then SIGKILL the process. Manifest doesn't reflect any of the 50 attempts.
findings.impact: Loss of diagnostic data when SEC is down. Operators can't tell which CIKs failed without re-running.
findings.suggested_fix: Flush manifest also after every N errors (e.g. `if ((errors) % 20 === 0)`). And keep an array of last-3 errors per CIK with timestamps, not just lastError.

findings.title: pull-earnings-dates: between-batch sleep ignores the last batch's tail time — can hammer Yahoo early after slow batch
findings.description: The for-loop sleeps 300ms between batches but does not account for how long Promise.all(batch) took. If a batch finishes in 2s, the next batch starts after 300ms (effective 2.3s/15 stocks = 153ms/stock). If a batch takes 50ms (cache hits), next batch fires at 350ms total — 23ms/stock — well over Yahoo's unspoken throttle. Yahoo eventually responds 429 mid-pull. The script has no retry/backoff for 429 — exception is swallowed silently in `.catch(() => {})`.
findings.mechanism: Line 38: `await Promise.all(batch.map(s => processOne(s).catch(() => {})))` — all errors swallowed, no detection of 429. Line 41: `await sleep(300)` — fixed, regardless of batch elapsed time.
findings.reproducer: Set EARNINGS_CONCURRENCY=15 and run when Yahoo is hot-cached (all responses <100ms). After ~30 batches, Yahoo returns 429 and 100s of tickers silently lose their earnings date.
findings.impact: Earnings calendar quietly under-reports upcoming earnings, leading to missed earnings-blackout filters in screener.
findings.suggested_fix: (1) Replace the silent .catch with one that logs failures by errClass and counts 429s separately. (2) On detecting 429, exponential backoff. (3) Use a token-bucket rate limiter instead of fixed batch sleep.

findings.title: threshold-audit findNumericChanges misses multi-line numeric changes and false-positives on adjacent unrelated diffs
findings.description: findNumericChanges() pairs `-` and `+` lines that are exactly adjacent in the diff. With `git show --unified=0`, hunks usually pair removed/added lines correctly, but renamed constants, comments inserted between them, or reformat-induced multi-line changes break the pairing. Worse: the regex only matches top-level `const NAME = N;` — assignments inside functions (`const RR = 0.4;` inside scoreMode()) are caught the same way, but `function foo(N=0.4) {}` defaults change is not caught at all. So changes to default parameters in function signatures escape audit.
findings.mechanism: Line 61-62: regex requires `^-(\s*const\s+\w+\s*=\s*)`. Default-param changes like `function score(stock, threshold = 0.4)` → `threshold = 0.5` produce diff lines that don't match the regex, are silently exempt.
findings.reproducer: Commit a single-character change to a default-param numeric in methods/foo.js without a discipline keyword. Run threshold-audit — no flag.
findings.impact: False sense of audit coverage. The audit catches the obvious case but misses parameter defaults, object-literal numeric fields, and ternary-branch constants.
findings.suggested_fix: Broaden the regex to also match `=` inside object/function-param contexts: `^[+-]\s*(?:const\s+\w+\s*=|.*=\s*)([+-]?\d+\.?\d*)`. Or use a proper JS parser (acorn) to diff numeric literals semantically. Document the audit's limitations explicitly in the README.

findings.title: threshold-audit --since arg is interpolated unsanitized into a shell command
findings.description: `SINCE = process.argv[process.argv.indexOf('--since') + 1]` is interpolated into `git log --since="${SINCE}"` and passed to execSync. A user-supplied --since value containing shell metacharacters (e.g. `--since '12.weeks;rm -rf .'`) would be executed. Not a remote-attack vector (CI input is trusted), but breaks the principle of least privilege and a typo with quote could destroy the repo.
findings.mechanism: Line 71: template-literal interpolation directly into shell-command string passed to execSync.
findings.reproducer: Run `node scripts/threshold-audit.js --since '1.week;echo OWNED'` — the echo executes.
findings.impact: Low (trust-domain is local CI). But it's a smell.
findings.suggested_fix: Validate SINCE matches `/^\d+\.(?:weeks?|days?|months?|years?)(?:\.ago)?$/` before interpolation. Or use execFileSync with argv array to skip shell.

findings.title: data-quality-report exchanges grouping treats 'undefined' meta exchange as 'Unknown' but doesn't normalize case/whitespace
findings.description: byExchange grouping uses `(s.meta && s.meta.exchange) || (s.meta && s.meta.exchangeCode) || 'Unknown'`. Two snapshots with exchange 'NMS' and 'nms' are counted as different exchanges; 'NMS ' (with trailing space) is a third. Yahoo's exchange field is inconsistent across regions. The top-20 list can be diluted by these case variants, hiding real distribution.
findings.mechanism: No .trim().toUpperCase() normalization before bucketing.
findings.reproducer: Mock two snapshots with 'NMS' and 'nms'. byExchange has both keys.
findings.impact: Minor reporting noise. Operator sees split rows in the report for the same exchange.
findings.suggested_fix: Normalize: `const exch = ((s.meta && (s.meta.exchange || s.meta.exchangeCode)) || 'Unknown').toString().trim().toUpperCase();`.

findings.title: pull-historical-prices keeps full history in memory and JSON.stringify(history, null, 2) — O(n) memory and slow write at scale
findings.description: history is loaded entirely into memory and rewritten at the end. With 5000 tickers * 400 days * ~50 bytes/entry, that's ~100 MB in memory + a single 100 MB writeFileSync. The pretty-printed JSON (`null, 2`) adds ~30% overhead. As universe grows, this becomes the dominant memory consumer in the pull workflow.
findings.mechanism: Lines 55-56: full parse into RAM. Line 142: full stringify+write at the end.
findings.reproducer: Grow watchlist to 10k tickers, observe RSS during write.
findings.impact: Future scalability. Not a current bug but a ceiling that will hit around 8-10k tickers.
findings.suggested_fix: Write per-ticker history files (prices/per-ticker/<safe>.json) instead of a single history.json. Or stream NDJSON.

findings.title: compute-method-drift: spread operator on potentially huge passVals array can crash on stack overflow
findings.description: `Math.min(...passVals)` and `Math.max(...passVals)` spread a potentially-large array as function arguments. The JS engine has a stack limit on argument count (~typically 65k-100k entries; v8 lowered to ~32k in some versions). With 1 vintage/day for 1000+ days, this would exceed the limit. Today the array is at most ~30 entries per method, so no current bug — but if vintage retention grows, this throws RangeError.
findings.mechanism: Spread of array > stack-arg-limit throws 'RangeError: Maximum call stack size exceeded'.
findings.reproducer: Mock passVals as 50000-element array. Math.min(...arr) throws.
findings.impact: Future-proofing. Currently fine.
findings.suggested_fix: Use reduce: `passVals.reduce((m, v) => v < m ? v : m, Infinity)`.

findings.title: compute-method-drift writes HTML to outputs/ but uses HTML special chars in methodId without escaping
findings.description: HTML row generation uses raw `${methodId}` in `${methodId}`. If a methodId ever contains `<`, `>`, `&`, `"`, or `'`, the HTML is corrupt or vulnerable to injection. Today methodIds are lowercase-kebab (no special chars), but the surrounding HTML/SVG construction is non-defensive.
findings.mechanism: Template-literal interpolation of methodId without htmlEscape. SVG points-attribute is constructed from numeric values — those are safe.
findings.reproducer: Add a methodId like 'risk-&-uncertainty'. Open method-drift.html — the `&` is preserved as-is, browser interprets as entity start.
findings.impact: Currently zero (no offending methodIds exist). Future risk if method registry adds unconventional IDs.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `function htmlEscape(s) { return String(s).replace(/[&<>"']/g, c => ({'&':'&','<':'<','>':'>','"':'"','\'':'''}[c])); }` and wrap interpolations.

findings.title: elliott-export & pick-diff HTML interpolate name/sector/ticker without escaping — XSS-by-data risk
findings.line_range: pick-diff.js:181-194; elliott-export.js:n/a HTML not generated but tickers from pick lists are placed in <span> via pick-diff
findings.description: pick-diff HTML generation interpolates `t` and `r.ticker` directly into `${t}` and table cells (lines 183, 190). Yahoo tickers normally don't contain HTML special chars, but defensive coding would escape them. The risk is amplified because pick-diff.html is committed to outputs/ and rendered locally by Karl — if an upstream snapshot loads a malformed ticker (e.g. via a watchlist mistake), the HTML is broken or interpretable as script.
findings.mechanism: Raw interpolation, no htmlEscape.
findings.reproducer: Add a watchlist entry with ticker ``. After pull and pick-diff run, the HTML contains the unescaped tag.
findings.impact: Low (no untrusted input source today) but principle-of-least-trust violation.
findings.suggested_fix: Add htmlEscape helper and wrap every ${} of user-controlled data.

findings.title: macro-regime: no minimum SMA period validation — series shorter than 200 days produces empty regimes silently
findings.description: computeRegimes skips dates `i < maPeriod - 1`. If the SPY series has only 100 entries (e.g. after a fresh history.json install), the function returns {} silently. main() logs `Regime dates computed: 0` and writes regimes:{} to the output. Downstream consumers (regime-overlay, walk-forward) then see no regime data and either default-fail-open or treat as 'BEAR' depending on their convention. There is no error, no warning that the input was too short.
findings.mechanism: Loop skips first 199 entries unconditionally; on series<200, all entries are skipped.
findings.reproducer: Create prices/history.json with only 100 SPY days. Run macro-regime.js. Output is regimes:{}, summary counts all 0, current:null is not even computed (no field exists).
findings.impact: Silent loss of regime overlay. Backtests assume regime is available, fall through to default branch, producing biased results.
findings.suggested_fix: After computeRegimes(), if `Object.keys(regimes).length === 0`, write an explicit error field (like the no-price-data branch does) and consider exit 1 in strict mode. Also log a warning when series.length < maPeriod.

findings.title: macro-regime computeRegimes recomputes SMA from scratch each iteration — O(n*p) where rolling-sum would be O(n)
findings.description: For each i, the inner loop sums p=200 elements. With 5000 days, that's ~1M additions. Rolling-window sum (subtract oldest, add newest) would be ~5k additions. Not a current bottleneck but trivially fixable.
findings.mechanism: Naive nested-loop SMA.
findings.reproducer: Time the function with 10k days. ~50ms vs <5ms rolling.
findings.impact: Negligible today, scales as O(n*p).
findings.suggested_fix: Maintain running sum: at i=maPeriod-1 compute the initial sum, then for i>=maPeriod, sum += series[i].close - series[i-maPeriod].close.

findings.title: pull-historical-prices: existing history.json parse failure silently zeros out history — risk of catastrophic data loss on bad write
findings.description: Lines 55-57: `try { history = JSON.parse(fs.readFileSync(histPath, 'utf8')); } catch (e) {}`. The catch block is empty — no log, no abort. If history.json was left corrupt by a prior SIGKILL or by the non-atomic write in pull-prices-bulk.js, this run silently resets history to {} and overwrites the corrupt-but-recoverable file with a fresh-empty version, destroying all backfilled history.
findings.mechanism: Empty catch swallows JSON.parse failure. Subsequent writeFileSync at line 142 then commits an effectively-empty history to disk.
findings.reproducer: Corrupt prices/history.json (truncate to first 100 bytes). Run pull-historical-prices.js. After the run, history.json contains only today's pulls — all back-history is gone.
findings.impact: Catastrophic loss of multi-year SPY/QQQ benchmark history. The pull repopulates 400 days, but anything older is unrecoverable from this file.
findings.suggested_fix: On parse failure: log error, copy histPath to histPath+'.corrupt.<ts>', and ABORT the run (not silently recover). Force operator to inspect.

findings.title: pull-earnings-dates main() lacks .catch — unhandled rejection on watchlist parse error crashes silently
findings.description: Line 47: `main();` is called without `.catch(...)`. If watchlist.json is missing or malformed, JSON.parse on line 18 throws synchronously — caught by the outer async function and surfaced as an unhandled promise rejection. In Node 15+, this defaults to crash, which is OK; in Node 14 it prints a warning and exits 0. Pipeline-health-check then sees no error and reports success.
findings.mechanism: main() throws inside async; no .catch attached at call site.
findings.reproducer: rm watchlist.json && node pull-earnings-dates.js. Behavior depends on Node version.
findings.impact: Inconsistent failure surfacing.
findings.suggested_fix: `main().catch(e => { console.error('pull-earnings-dates failed: ' + e.message); process.exit(1); });`.

findings.title: pick-diff rollingJaccard: window string says '4w' but reflects up-to-28 daily snapshots — misleading if pipeline runs <daily
findings.description: Comment says 'up to 28 daily snapshots ≈ 4 calendar weeks' but the pipeline runs once per CI invocation. If the CI cadence drops to 3x/week (which it can — see commit history of yahoo-pull commits), the rolling-4w window covers ~9 weeks of actual calendar time. The label '4w' is hard-coded and never reflects reality.
findings.mechanism: slice(-28) is by vintage count, not by calendar date. Label is static.
findings.reproducer: If pipeline runs weekly, rollingJaccard.window says '4w' but actually covers 28 weeks.
findings.impact: Operator misreads the metric. Stability assessment is based on wrong assumption of frequency.
findings.suggested_fix: Derive window from actual date range: `const oldest = recentVintages[0], newest = recentVintages.at(-1); window = daysBetween(oldest, newest)+'d';`. Or restrict slice to only the last 28 days of dates, not the last 28 files.

findings.title: elliott-export skips snapshot evaluation when snapshot is missing but still emits row with empty fields — diluted output
findings.description: When loadSnapshot returns null, the script emits a row with most fields blank but the ticker/score/etc. from picks-history. Operator can't easily tell from the CSV which rows have complete vs partial data. The dq_grade is left blank (line 93), but other consumers may not check that. A `_partial: true` marker column would be clearer.
findings.mechanism: No flag column distinguishes degraded rows.
findings.reproducer: Remove a snapshot file, re-run. CSV contains a row with empty technical fields and no marker.
findings.impact: Operator may rank-sort by drawdown_52w and see the partial rows clustering at 'no data' position, looking like real values.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `data_complete` column (true/false) or skip the row when snapshot is missing and emit a separate skipped.csv.

findings.title: data-quality-report: gradeSnapshot fallback masks methods/data-quality.js failures
findings.description: If methods/data-quality.js fails to load (syntax error, missing dependency), the catch block silently substitutes `() => ({ grade: 'unknown', nanRatio: 0, missingFields: [] })`. The whole report then shows 100% Unknown grade, but the markdown output looks structurally correct. There is no warning that the grader is non-functional.
findings.mechanism: Line 19: silent fallback. Line 101: gradeSnapshot(s) returns unknown for every snapshot.
findings.reproducer: Rename methods/data-quality.js. Run data-quality-report.js. Output looks normal but with all snapshots in 'unknown' grade.
findings.impact: Silent loss of quality signal. Operator may not notice 'unknown' grade dominating until much later.
findings.suggested_fix: On require failure, log the error AND exit 1 (or write the report with a top-line warning banner).

findings.title: pick-diff rollingJaccard reads up to 28 picks-history files synchronously inside main() — slow with future growth
findings.description: For each of 3 modes, the function re-reads up to 28 picks-history JSON files (~65 KB each). That's 84 file reads + 84 JSON.parse calls. Trivially fast today but scales linearly with mode count if more modes are added.
findings.mechanism: Nested loops: mode * vintages. Could read each vintage once outside the mode loop.
findings.reproducer: Add 10 more modes — performance scales linearly.
findings.impact: Future scaling.
findings.suggested_fix: Read each vintage once, build a map vintage->modes->tickers, then iterate modes against the prebuilt map.
